questions.question: In the Sales Velocity equation, which variable is often the easiest for a solo founder to optimize for rapid revenue growth?

options.text: Length of Sales Cycle (Reducing the time between first contact and close).

questions.explanation: Increasing volume or value often requires significant marketing or product shifts. Reducing cycle length by removing internal friction (Legal/Security prep) allows you to process more deals through your limited time as a founder.

questions.question: What does a high 'Leakage Rate' in the middle of your pipeline usually indicate?

options.text: A 'Qualification Gap'—you are allowing unqualified prospects into the pipeline, causing effort to be wasted on deals that will never close.

questions.explanation: If deals fall out early, it means your ICP/Discovery filters aren't sharp enough. Tightening qualification reduces the number of deals but increases the quality and velocity of the ones that remain.

questions.question: What is the 'Inverted Hourglass' pipeline shape?

options.text: A pipeline that is narrow at the top and wide at the bottom.
A model where you have broad outreach (Top), brutal qualification (Middle), and deep expansion/upselling (Bottom).
A pipeline that runs out of time.
A pipeline where everyone is a high-value whale.

questions.explanation: In 2026, the real profit for solo founders is in high-trust expansion. The'Inverted Hourglass' emphasizes that the journey doesn't end at the first sale; the widest part of your revenue should come from growing accounts.

questions.question: Calculate your current 'Sales Velocity' using rough numbers. (Oreo x Deal Value x Win Rate / Cycle Length). Which one of these 4 variables, if improved by just 10%, would have the biggest impact on your stress levels AND your bank account? Why?
questions.aiPrompt: Check if the student can accurately apply the velocity formula and if they prioritize'Cycle Length' or'Win Rate' as a way to reduce founder effort while maintaining revenue.
